ideas.intro: Ideas for projects, activities, books, games, and more for students with a high level of interest.

items.content:
[ ] Make your own "My Fairy Tales" and/ or "Tall Tales" book together.
Let student dictate what they want you to write as narration of the scene or as captions to their brush drawings (see Afternoon Occupations). Bind the illustrations and narrations into a story book.
[ ] Act out favorite stories for family and friends.
[ ] Look for local theater or dance companies performing these stories.

[ ] Look for different versions of a classic fairy tale like Cinderella or Goldilocks at your library. Compare the versions and discuss what you like best about each.
[ ] Ask family and friends about their favorite fairy tale or tall tale. Are any mentioned more than once? Are there any you would like to read?
[ ] Create a new ending (or an entirely different plot) for a fairy tale or tall tale you have read.

[ ] Use blocks, Legos, cardboard, or other building materials to create a scene from a fairy tale or tall tale you have read.
[ ] Create a wanted poster for a villain from one of the tales. What was their crime? How can they be identified? What reward will be given for their capture? Be sure to include a picture.
[ ] Retell a story you have read from a different point of view. For example, you could tell a story about Paul Bunyan from the point of view of Babe or his axe.

resources.title: An Illustrated Treasury of Hans Christian Andersen's Fairy Tales
resources.author: Hans Christian Andersen
resources.rationale: More fairy tale readings. Check local library for picture book versions of these Grimm or Andersen tales. There are often multiple versions to read and compare to the anthology version.

resources.title: Favorite Folk Tales from Around the World
resources.author: Jane Yolen

resources.title: Favorite Folktales from Around the World
resources.author: Jane Yolen

resources.title: The Egyptian Cinderella
resources.author: Shirley Climo
resources.rationale: 1 of 4 Cinderella picture books from Persia, Egypt, Ireland, and Korea.

resources.title: Value of Folktales
resources.author: Rebecca Sheir
resources.rationale: This podcast episode is a wonderful teacher resource on the value of folktales. Rebecca Sheir shares how folktales entertain, inspire, and unite us in this noisy, high-tech world, and act as a bridge between cultures and communities.
